community_involvement.community_involvement_activity_1:
Tempe Community Food Coalition – Steering Committee Member (2018–Present)

I serve on the steering committee of the Tempe Community Food Coalition, a collaborative of local nonprofits, faith communities, schools, and small businesses focused on food security and nutrition education in Tempe. I joined initially as a volunteer in late 2017 and was invited to the steering committee in 2018 after consistently helping coordinate weekend food distributions.

Role & responsibilities:
- Co-coordinate our monthly community food distribution at Escalante Community Center, including volunteer recruitment, training, and day-of logistics.
- Lead data tracking for our distributions (households served, pounds of food distributed, repeat vs. new households, and basic demographic information when voluntarily provided).
- Serve as liaison between the coalition and two neighborhood schools (Carminati Elementary and Gililland Middle School) to align our weekend food backpack program with school social workers’ assessments of family needs.
- Help write small local grant applications (e.g., from the Tempe Diablos and local corporate giving programs) and provide outcome data and narratives for grant reports.

Projects undertaken:
- Expanded weekend food backpack program: In 2019, I led a project team of five volunteers to expand our Friday backpack program from serving one elementary school to three (Carminati, Aguilar, and Holdeman). I coordinated meetings with school principals and social workers, created a simple needs-assessment form, and designed a packing and delivery schedule that could be sustained by volunteers.
- Nutrition and cooking workshops: In 2021–2022, I worked with a local dietitian and a bilingual (English/Spanish) volunteer to pilot a series of four “Cooking on a Budget” workshops. I helped with curriculum planning, created handouts in both English and Spanish, and managed registration and follow-up surveys.

Measures of success:
- Quantitative: Since joining the steering committee, our monthly distribution has grown from serving roughly 80–100 households per month to a consistent 220–260 households per month as of 2024, while maintaining an average of 18–25 volunteers per distribution.
- School impact: The backpack program now consistently serves about 140 students per week across three schools during the school year, up from roughly 35 students when I first became involved. School social workers have reported fewer Monday-morning nurse visits related to hunger or headaches among identified students, which they specifically attribute to improved weekend food access.
- Sustainability: We have secured multi-year commitments from two local grocery stores for regular surplus donations, which I helped formalize through simple MOUs. This has reduced our reliance on one-time food drives and made planning more predictable.
- Community feedback: Through anonymous surveys distributed in English and Spanish, 87% of respondents reported that receiving food through the coalition helped them “a lot” with stretching their monthly food budget; 72% reported feeling “more connected” to neighborhood resources after attending at least two distributions. I helped design and analyze these surveys.

Personally, this activity matters to me because I grew up in a household that occasionally relied on food pantries when my dad was laid off, and I remember the mix of gratitude and embarrassment that came with that. Being able to design services that are dignified, welcoming, and culturally respectful is central to how I measure my own success in this role.
community_involvement.community_involvement_activity_2:
City of Tempe Neighborhood Association – Board Member & Block Captain (2016–Present)

I serve on the board of the Hudson Manor Neighborhood Association, a small but active neighborhood just north of the US-60 in Tempe, and I also act as a block captain for my immediate street.

Role & responsibilities:
- Elected board member since 2018; previously an at-large volunteer (2016–2018).
- Help plan and facilitate quarterly neighborhood meetings, including inviting city staff (transportation, code compliance, police, sustainability office) to speak on topics of interest.
- Coordinate our annual neighborhood clean-up and alley beautification day in partnership with the City’s Neighborhood Services Office.
- Manage the neighborhood’s email newsletter and co-manage the private Facebook group, focusing on sharing accurate information about city services, upcoming hearings, and community resources.
- Act as a block captain: I welcome new residents on my block with a printed resource sheet that includes trash/recycling schedules, key city phone numbers, and a list of local parks and libraries. I also maintain a simple contact list (opt-in) for my block for emergency communication.

Projects undertaken:
- Traffic calming initiative: In 2019–2020, I helped organize a neighborhood petition and data-gathering effort around speeding on our main cut-through street. I coordinated with the city’s transportation department to conduct speed studies, shared the results at a neighborhood meeting, and facilitated a consensus process around preferred traffic calming options. The project ultimately led to the installation of two speed humps and improved crosswalk markings near a bus stop.
- Alley beautification and alley light mapping: In 2021, I led a team of neighbors to map out non-functioning alley lights and areas with frequent illegal dumping. I compiled photos and a simple map, submitted them to the city, and followed up regularly until repairs and clean-up occurred. We then organized a community painting day to add murals to several alley walls with the permission of property owners.
- Emergency preparedness workshop: In 2022, I collaborated with the Tempe Fire Medical Rescue Department to host a neighborhood emergency preparedness workshop. I handled outreach, logistics, and post-event evaluation, and then summarized key takeaways in a bilingual handout for neighbors who couldn’t attend.

Measures of success:
- Participation: Quarterly meeting attendance has grown from about 10–12 residents to a consistent 25–30, with more renters and younger families participating. I track attendance and note demographics informally to see if we’re reaching beyond the “usual suspects.”
- Measurable city improvements: Our traffic calming project led to a recorded reduction in average vehicle speeds by about 7 mph on our main street, according to city follow-up studies. Neighbors, especially parents with children who bike to school, have reported feeling safer.
- Communication reach: Our email list has grown from roughly 45 to over 130 households, and our Facebook group participation has doubled. Open rates for the newsletter hover around 60–65%, which I interpret as a strong engagement indicator.
- Relationship-building: My personal measure of success is that neighbors now feel comfortable reaching out to me when they have questions about city processes, from code issues to how to apply for the city’s water conservation rebates. I see this trust as a key outcome of consistent, honest, and transparent communication over several years.

This involvement has helped me understand city structures and decision-making processes in a hands-on way, which is one of the reasons I’m particularly interested in the Tempe Leadership program—to deepen that understanding and be more effective in advocating for our neighborhood and others like it.
community_involvement.community_involvement_activity_3:
Tempe Youth Mentoring & Career Readiness – Volunteer Mentor (2019–Present)

Since 2019, I have volunteered as a mentor with a local nonprofit that partners with Tempe Union High School District to support juniors and seniors who are first-generation college-bound or exploring non-traditional post-secondary paths (trade schools, apprenticeships, etc.). I was matched with my first mentee in fall 2019 and have since mentored four students, continuing to stay in touch with two of them as they transition into college and the workforce.

Role & responsibilities:
- Meet one-on-one with my mentee for at least two hours per month during the school year, usually split between in-person meetings (at the school or a public space like the Tempe Public Library) and video calls.
- Help students identify their strengths, interests, and potential career paths through guided conversations and simple assessments.
- Assist with practical tasks: college and scholarship applications, FAFSA completion, resume drafting, basic interview practice, and time-management planning for balancing school, work, and family responsibilities.
- Communicate as needed with program staff and, when appropriate and with the student’s consent, with school counselors to ensure we’re aligned on academic requirements and deadlines.

Projects undertaken:
- College & career workshop series: In 2020–2021, when COVID-19 disrupted in-person programming, I volunteered to help the program pivot to virtual workshops. I co-designed and facilitated three online sessions: “Telling Your Story in Applications,” “Intro to Financial Aid and Scholarships,” and “Exploring Community College and Transfer Options.” I created slide decks, compiled resource lists, and helped troubleshoot technology issues for students joining from phones or older computers.
- Scholarship resource guide: In 2022, I took the initiative to consolidate scattered scholarship information into a simple, Tempe-focused guide that highlighted local scholarships from community organizations, city programs, and local businesses. I organized it by eligibility (e.g., first-generation, specific majors, community service involvement) and updated it mid-year with new opportunities.

Measures of success:
- Individual student outcomes: Of the four students I’ve mentored directly, two are currently enrolled at Mesa Community College with plans to transfer to ASU, one is in a medical assistant training program at a local technical school, and one is working full-time while taking evening classes. All four successfully completed high school and had at least one concrete post-graduation plan in place by May of their senior year.
- Skill development: I track qualitative progress in areas like confidence speaking to adults, ability to advocate for themselves with teachers or employers, and capacity to manage deadlines. For example, one mentee who was initially very shy led part of a Q&A panel for younger students by the end of our time together, which we both celebrated as a major personal milestone.
- Program contribution: The virtual workshop content and scholarship guide I developed are still being used and updated by program staff, which tells me that the time I invested had lasting value beyond my direct mentees.

My personal measure of success is when a student I’ve worked with feels comfortable reaching out to me months later to share an update or ask for advice, not because they have to, but because they see me as part of their long-term support network. This activity keeps me grounded in the real experiences of young people in our community and reminds me that leadership is often about consistent, behind-the-scenes support rather than being in the spotlight.
